text:
NUCLEAR MEDICINE / RADIOLOGY
BONE SCINTIGRAPHY AND COMPUTED TOMOGRAPHY — ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH CONTRAST
Response Assessment Study

PATIENT: Raymond Kowalski
MRN: [REDACTED]
CLINICAL INDICATION: Metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer. Patient currently on enzalutamide 160 mg daily with continued ADT (leuprolide). Prior history of disease progression on abiraterone/ADT, with biochemical castration resistance confirmed. Presenting PSA nadir on enzalutamide of 0.6 ng/mL. Study ordered for response assessment and comparison with restaging PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 5 months prior, which demonstrated progressive osseous and nodal disease.

COMPARISON: Ga-68 PSMA-11 PET/CT (restaging, performed at CRPC progression on abiraterone); technetium-99m bone scan (12 months prior to current study); CT abdomen/pelvis (12 months prior to current study).

---

PART I: TECHNETIUM-99M METHYLENE DIPHOSPHONATE (MDP) WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY

TECHNIQUE: Intravenous administration of approximately 25 mCi of Tc-99m MDP. Whole-body planar anterior and posterior images obtained at 3 hours post-injection. SPECT/CT images obtained through the lumbar spine, pelvis, and right hip girdle. Adequate tracer distribution. Good image quality. No significant bladder artifact obscuring pelvic bony structures.

FINDINGS:

AXIAL SKELETON — SPINE:
At the L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, foci of increased radiotracer uptake persist but appear notably less intense compared with the prior restaging PSMA PET/CT performed at the time of castration resistance transition. The areas of uptake have a more focal, sharply defined quality compared with the more diffuse, confluent pattern of avidity appreciated on prior cross-sectional studies. Correlative SPECT/CT images demonstrate dense sclerotic changes at these levels with cortical thickening, which is the expected morphological correlate of healing/treated osseous metastatic disease. No new or enlarging foci of abnormal uptake are identified at L1-L2 or elsewhere in the lumbar spine.

At T10, a focus of mildly increased uptake persists. On SPECT/CT, this corresponds to a small area of subtle sclerosis in the posterior vertebral body — appearances consistent with a treated lesion and overall stable compared with the most recent prior bone scan, without convincing evidence of interval growth or new soft tissue component.

No abnormal uptake identified in the cervical spine, thoracic spine above T10, or the remaining thoracolumbar levels.

AXIAL SKELETON — PELVIS AND SACRUM:
At the right sacral ala, a previously documented focus of tracer uptake is again identified and appears similar in intensity to the prior examination, with no significant interval change in size or character. Correlative SPECT/CT shows a small area of sclerosis, without cortical disruption or soft tissue mass. Appearances favor a treated, stable osseous lesion.

The right iliac wing demonstrates persistent but diminished focal uptake corresponding to a sclerotic lesion on CT — this sclerosis is more pronounced than on prior imaging, compatible with progressive mineralization in the setting of a favorable treatment response.

No new abnormal foci of uptake identified in the sacrum, pubic symphysis, ischial rami, or left hemipelvis.

APPENDICULAR SKELETON — RIGHT HIP AND FEMUR:
A focus of mildly increased uptake persists at the right femoral neck, corresponding to sclerotic change on SPECT/CT. The uptake appears less intense and more confined than on the antecedent bone scan. No cortical irregularity or periosteal reaction to suggest mechanical compromise or impending pathological fracture at this site. No new lesion at the right femoral head or intertrochanteric region.

RIBS:
Previously identified foci at the right posterior 7th rib and left posterior 9th rib demonstrate persistent but diminished radiotracer uptake. The right posterior 7th rib focus is characterized by focal sclerosis on correlative CT without frank cortical disruption. The left 9th rib lesion similarly has a sclerotic morphology. No new rib lesions are appreciated on planar whole-body images or on spot views of the thoracic cage.

CALVARIUM, STERNUM, SHOULDERS, LONG BONES:
No convincing areas of pathologically increased radiotracer uptake are identified in the calvarium, clavicles, sternum, scapulae, humeri, radii, ulnae, or lower extremities beyond the right femoral neck lesion noted above. Faint increased uptake in the bilateral knees is symmetric and correlates with mild degenerative changes on CT — a benign finding.

SOFT TISSUES:
Appropriate renal excretion and clearance of tracer. Bladder activity present. No abnormal soft tissue tracer uptake. No superscan pattern.

BONE SCAN SUMMARY:
All previously documented sites of osseous metastatic disease — including L3 and L4 vertebral bodies, T10 vertebral body, right posterior 7th rib, left posterior 9th rib, right sacral ala, right iliac wing, and right femoral neck — are again identified but demonstrate uniformly diminished radiotracer avidity with accompanying sclerotic morphology on correlative SPECT/CT. These findings are characteristic of an osteoblastic healing response, highly consistent with favorable treatment effect in the setting of known prostatic metastases. No new osseous lesions are identified.

---

PART II: CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE: Axial CT images of the abdomen and pelvis were acquired following the intravenous administration of 100 mL of iodinated contrast material (portal venous phase). 5 mm axial slices with multiplanar reformats in the coronal and sagittal planes. No oral contrast administered per departmental protocol. BMI-appropriate parameters utilized. Adequate image quality without significant motion artifact.

ABDOMEN:

LIVER AND BILIARY:
The liver is smooth in contour and homogeneous in attenuation throughout both lobes. No focal hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilation. The gallbladder is surgically absent (prior cholecystectomy per available medical history). No perihepatic fluid.

SPLEEN:
Mildly enlarged spleen measuring approximately 13.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension, similar to prior. No focal splenic lesions. This likely relates to the patient's ongoing systemic treatment and/or physiologic variation.

PANCREAS:
The pancreas is normal in attenuation and morphology without ductal dilation or peripancreatic stranding. No pancreatic mass. (Of note, patient has a family history of pancreatic cancer in a first-degree relative; no pancreatic abnormality identified on this study.)

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear symmetric and unremarkable. No adrenal mass or thickening.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
The kidneys are normal in size and morphology bilaterally. Symmetric cortical enhancement. No hydronephrosis. No renal calculi. No perinephric fat stranding. No ureteral dilation.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:
Bowel loops are non-dilated throughout the imaged abdomen. No transition point. No pneumatosis or free air. The colon is decompressed without filling defects or wall thickening.

ASCITES / PERITONEUM:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental thickening to suggest carcinomatosis.

ABDOMINAL AORTA AND VESSELS:
Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the infrarenal aorta, appropriate for age. No aneurysm. No significant stenosis identified.

ABDOMINAL LYMPH NODES:
No pathologically enlarged lymph nodes are identified in the retroperitoneum, mesenteric root, or upper abdominal paraaortic chains.

PELVIS:

PROSTATE / SEMINAL VESICLE BED:
Post-treatment changes in the prostatic bed are noted. The prostate gland is markedly enlarged and heterogeneous, measuring approximately 50 cc, with irregular margins and low-attenuation areas consistent with previously documented extracapsular extension and seminal vesicle involvement. The seminal vesicle region is distorted, consistent with known invasion. No new presacral or periprostatic mass identified.

BLADDER:
The urinary bladder is moderately distended. Bladder wall is smooth without focal nodularity or thickening.

RECTUM AND SIGMOID:
The rectum and sigmoid colon are unremarkable without mural thickening or perirectal stranding.

PELVIC LYMPH NODES:
Right obturator lymph node: this node, which has been a consistent site of lymphadenopathy throughout the patient's disease course, now measures 0.9 cm in short axis on the current study. This represents a notable decrease from 1.4 cm at the time of restaging PSMA PET/CT performed approximately 5 months ago, and from 2.1 cm at the nadir of prior abiraterone progression. The node appears ovoid in configuration with some preserved fatty hilum, indicating meaningful interval reduction in size consistent with nodal treatment response.

No other pelvic lymph nodes meet size criteria for pathologic enlargement. The bilateral common, external, and internal iliac, inguinal, and perirectal lymph node stations are unremarkable.

BONY PELVIS (CT CORRELATION):
Sclerotic changes are again noted throughout the right iliac wing, right sacral ala, and portions of the right acetabulum. These appear more confluently sclerotic and dense compared with prior studies, compatible with continued osseous healing response rather than progressive lytic disease. No pathological fracture or significant cortical disruption. Right femoral neck demonstrates focal sclerosis without impending pathological fracture features.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. OSSEOUS METASTATIC DISEASE — FAVORABLE TREATMENT RESPONSE: All previously documented skeletal metastases (L3, L4, T10 vertebral bodies; right posterior 7th rib; left posterior 9th rib; right sacral ala; right iliac wing; right femoral neck) demonstrate diminished radiotracer uptake on scintigraphy accompanied by progressive sclerotic changes on CT. The pattern is characteristic of an osteoblastic healing response. No new osseous lesions are identified anywhere in the axial or appendicular skeleton.

2. RIGHT OBTURATOR LYMPH NODE — INTERVAL DECREASE: The index right obturator lymph node has decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm in short axis, representing a meaningful interval reduction in size and consistent with nodal response to enzalutamide therapy.

3. NO VISCERAL METASTASES: No hepatic, pulmonary (below the lung bases visible on upper abdominal CT cuts), peritoneal, adrenal, or other visceral metastatic deposits identified.

4. NO NEW LYMPHADENOPATHY: No new pathologically enlarged lymph nodes in the abdomen or pelvis.

Overall, the scintigraphic and CT findings are consistent with a meaningful response to enzalutamide, with stabilization and partial treatment response across both osseous and nodal disease sites.

Dictated and electronically signed by:
Attending Radiologist / Nuclear Medicine Physician
[Signature block redacted]
Department of Diagnostic Radiology and Nuclear Medicine
